name: planner
content:
**Plan:**

- Step 1: DNS Simulation (GPU)
     * sub-task: Run the 2D turbulence DNS simulation with passive tracer advection
     * agent: engineer
     * bullet points:
       - Copy `/home/node/work/projects/levy_flights_2dns_v2/dns_template_1024_ekman.py` to `codebase/step_1.py`
       - Modify ONLY the production loop to save all required output files to `/home/node/work/projects/levy_flights_2dns_v2/data/`
       - Keep all solver parameters from the template (N=1024, k_force=[20,40], lambda_ekman=0.5, nu_h=3.9e-31, T_spinup=60, dt_snap=0.02)
       - Save: tracer_positions.npy, tracer_velocities.npy, tracer_times.npy, velocity_snapshots.npy (coarsened 1024→256), vorticity_snapshots.npy, vel_times.npy, energy_spectrum.npy, diagnostics.npy (structured array with fields: time, E_total, E_rms, omega_rms, T_L, k_peak, d_vv_estimate), sim_params.json
       - Print U_rms, T_L, k_peak after spinup and every 10 T_L during production
       - ALL tensor operations must remain on device='cuda'
     * code_execution_timeout: 7200

- Step 2: Data Quality Verification and Inverse Cascade Confirmation
     * sub-task: Verify snapshot quality and confirm inverse cascade development
     * agent: engineer
     * bullet points:
       - Load tracer_positions.npy, tracer_velocities.npy, tracer_times.npy, energy_spectrum.npy, diagnostics.npy from the data directory
       - Compute mean tracer displacement per snapshot step and check it is < 0.3 × 2π ≈ 1.88; print result and flag if violated
       - Compute VACF C_vv(τ) at lags τ = 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 steps; report C_vv(τ=1) and decorrelation time τ_corr
       - Plot E(k) at t = 0.1, 0.3, 0.6, 1.0 × T_prod on a single figure with 4 subplots (log-log scale), showing k_peak drift from forcing scale toward large scales
       - Plot k_peak(t) vs time to visualize inverse cascade progression
       - Save all plots to the data directory
     * code_execution_timeout: 600

- Step 3: Non-Stationarity Analysis — Computation
     * sub-task: Compute α(t), γ(t), ν(t), d_vv(t), k_peak(t) in overlapping temporal windows
     * agent: engineer
     * bullet points:
       - Load tracer_positions.npy, tracer_velocities.npy, tracer_times.npy, diagnostics.npy
       - Define overlapping windows of width W = 8 T_L, step ΔW = 2 T_L over the production run
       - For each window: fit Lévy-stable α via McCulloch quantile method (using quantiles p5, p25, p50, p75, p95 of displacement distribution at lags τ = {0.5, 1, 2} T_L); also compute Hill estimator as cross-check
       - For each window: fit MSD(τ) power law to get γ(t); fit VACF algebraic decay to get ν(t)
       - Extract d_vv(t) and k_peak(t) from diagnostics averaged within each window
       - Run ADF and KPSS stationarity tests on α(t) and γ(t) time series
       - Save all window-level results (α, γ, ν, d_vv, k_peak, window center times) to a numpy/npz file
       - Print all stationarity test statistics and p-values
     * code_execution_timeout: 1800

- Step 4: Non-Stationarity Analysis — Visualization
     * sub-task: Plot non-stationarity results and α vs k_peak relationship
     * agent: engineer
     * bullet points:
       - Load the window-level results saved in Step 3
       - Plot α(t) and γ(t) vs time (with McCulloch and Hill α on same panel), showing temporal evolution
       - Plot ν(t), d_vv(t), k_peak(t) vs time in a multi-panel figure
       - Plot α(t) vs k_peak(t)/k_box (where k_box=1) and fit α = a + b × (k_peak/k_box)^c; show fit on plot
       - Save all plots to the data directory
     * code_execution_timeout: 300

- Step 5: Gaussian Band-Pass Wavelet Analysis — Computation
     * sub-task: Filter velocity fields into Gaussian spectral bands and re-advect tracers
     * agent: engineer
     * bullet points:
       - Load velocity_snapshots.npy (shape N_vel, 2, 256, 256), vel_times.npy, tracer_positions.npy, tracer_times.npy from the data directory
       - Define 5 Gaussian band-pass filters in k-space: (k0=25,σ=5), (k0=15,σ=4), (k0=8,σ=3), (k0=4,σ=2), (k0=2,σ=1)
       - For each band: apply rfft2 on GPU, multiply by Gaussian filter G(k)=exp(-(|k|-k0)²/(2σ²)), irfft2 to get filtered velocity fields
       - For each band: compute T_eddy(j) from temporal autocorrelation of band energy (integrate until C_E drops below 0.1)
       - For each band: re-advect 2000 tracers (subset of saved positions) using filtered velocity fields with RK4 integration and GPU grid_sample interpolation
       - For each band: compute MSD exponent γ(j), VACF decay ν(j), and displacement CCDF slope (proxy for α)
       - Save all band results (T_eddy per band, α/γ/ν per band, CCDF slopes) to npz file
       - Print all numerical results per band
     * code_execution_timeout: 3600

- Step 6: Wavelet Analysis — Visualization and CTRW Test
     * sub-task: Plot band-pass wavelet results and test CTRW scaling prediction
     * agent: engineer
     * bullet points:
       - Load band results from Step 5
       - Plot α(j) (CCDF slope proxy) vs T_eddy(j) for all 5 bands; fit power-law or linear relationship
       - Plot γ(j) vs T_eddy(j) for all 5 bands
       - Test CTRW prediction: compute β(j) from α(j) via α = 1 + β/(2−β), plot β(j) vs T_eddy(j) and fit β ∝ T_eddy^δ; report δ
       - Plot CCDF of displacement magnitudes for each band on a single log-log figure to show tail behavior
       - Save all plots to the data directory
     * code_execution_timeout: 300

- Step 7: Synthesis — Computation
     * sub-task: Compare temporal α(t) with band α(j) and build 2D map
     * agent: engineer
     * bullet points:
       - Load window-level results from Step 3 and band results from Step 5
       - For each temporal window, identify the dominant wavelet band j_peak(t) as the band whose k0 is closest to k_peak(t)
       - Compare α(t) from Step 3 with α(j_peak(t)) from Step 5 — compute correlation and mean difference
       - Build a 2D scatter/interpolated map of α as a function of (T_eddy, k_peak/k_box) using all available data points from both analyses
       - Save synthesis results (comparison arrays, 2D map data) to npz file
       - Print all numerical comparison statistics
     * code_execution_timeout: 300

- Step 8: Synthesis — Visualization
     * sub-task: Generate synthesis plots
     * agent: engineer
     * bullet points:
       - Load synthesis results from Step 7
       - Plot α(t) from temporal windows vs α(j_peak(t)) from wavelet bands as a scatter plot with 1:1 line
       - Plot the 2D map of α as a function of (T_eddy, k_peak/k_box) using a color-coded scatter or interpolated contour plot
       - Plot α(t) overlaid with k_peak(t)/k_box on a dual-axis time series to visually show co-evolution
       - Save all plots to the data directory
     * code_execution_timeout: 300

- Step 9: Results Report
     * sub-task: Write the full Results section of the paper
     * agent: researcher
     * bullet points:
       - Summarize all quantitative results from Steps 2–8: displacement quality check, VACF decorrelation, inverse cascade confirmation, stationarity test outcomes, α(t)/γ(t)/ν(t) evolution, α vs k_peak fit parameters, band-pass T_eddy values, CTRW scaling exponent δ, synthesis comparison
       - Interpret the physical meaning of each result in the context of Lévy flight theory, CTRW, and 2D turbulence inverse cascade
       - Discuss whether α ≈ 1.4 from v1 is stationary or drifts, and what drives the drift
       - Discuss the CTRW prediction α = 1 + β/(2−β) and whether it holds across wavelet bands
       - Reference all plots generated in previous steps by description
       - Write in academic paper style with full quantitative detail

name: planner_response_evaluator
content:

	* instructions:

		- Modify ONLY the production loop to save all required output files to /home/node/work/projects/levy_flights_2dns_v2/data/
		- Keep all solver parameters from the template (N=1024, k_force=[20,40], lambda_ekman=0.5, nu_h=3.9e-31, T_spinup=60, dt_snap=0.02)
		- Save: tracer_positions.npy, tracer_velocities.npy, tracer_times.npy, velocity_snapshots.npy (coarsened 1024→256), vorticity_snapshots.npy, vel_times.npy, energy_spectrum.npy, diagnostics.npy (structured array with fields: time, E_total, E_rms, omega_rms, T_L, k_peak, d_vv_estimate), sim_params.json

		- ALL tensor operations must remain on device='cuda'

- Step 2:
	* sub-task: Verify snapshot quality and confirm inverse cascade development
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* code_execution_timeout: 600s

	* instructions:
		- Load tracer_positions.npy, tracer_velocities.npy, tracer_times.npy, energy_spectrum.npy, diagnostics.npy from the data directory
		- Compute mean tracer displacement per snapshot step and check it is < 0.3 × 2π ≈ 1.88; print result and flag if violated
		- Compute VACF C_vv(τ) at lags τ = 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 steps; report C_vv(τ=1) and decorrelation time τ_corr
		- Plot E(k) at t = 0.1, 0.3, 0.6, 1.0 × T_prod on a single figure with 4 subplots (log-log scale), showing k_peak drift from forcing scale toward large scales
		- Plot k_peak(t) vs time to visualize inverse cascade progression
		- Save all plots to the data directory

- Step 3:
	* sub-task: Compute α(t), γ(t), ν(t), d_vv(t), k_peak(t) in overlapping temporal windows
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* code_execution_timeout: 1800s

	* instructions:
		- Load tracer_positions.npy, tracer_velocities.npy, tracer_times.npy, diagnostics.npy
		- Define overlapping windows of width W = 8 T_L, step ΔW = 2 T_L over the production run
		- For each window: fit Lévy-stable α via McCulloch quantile method (using quantiles p5, p25, p50, p75, p95 of displacement distribution at lags τ = {0.5, 1, 2} T_L); also compute Hill estimator as cross-check
		- For each window: fit MSD(τ) power law to get γ(t); fit VACF algebraic decay to get ν(t)
		- Extract d_vv(t) and k_peak(t) from diagnostics averaged within each window
		- Run ADF and KPSS stationarity tests on α(t) and γ(t) time series
		- Save all window-level results (α, γ, ν, d_vv, k_peak, window center times) to a numpy/npz file
		- Print all stationarity test statistics and p-values

- Step 4:
	* sub-task: Plot non-stationarity results and α vs k_peak relationship
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* code_execution_timeout: 300s

	* instructions:
		- Load the window-level results saved in Step 3
		- Plot α(t) and γ(t) vs time (with McCulloch and Hill α on same panel), showing temporal evolution
		- Plot ν(t), d_vv(t), k_peak(t) vs time in a multi-panel figure
		- Plot α(t) vs k_peak(t)/k_box (where k_box=1) and fit α = a + b × (k_peak/k_box)^c; show fit on plot
		- Save all plots to the data directory

- Step 5:
	* sub-task: Filter velocity fields into Gaussian spectral bands and re-advect tracers
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* code_execution_timeout: 3600s

	* instructions:
		- Load velocity_snapshots.npy (shape N_vel, 2, 256, 256), vel_times.npy, tracer_positions.npy, tracer_times.npy from the data directory
		- Define 5 Gaussian band-pass filters in k-space: (k0=25,σ=5), (k0=15,σ=4), (k0=8,σ=3), (k0=4,σ=2), (k0=2,σ=1)
		- For each band: apply rfft2 on GPU, multiply by Gaussian filter G(k)=exp(-(|k|-k0)²/(2σ²)), irfft2 to get filtered velocity fields
		- For each band: compute T_eddy(j) from temporal autocorrelation of band energy (integrate until C_E drops below 0.1)
		- For each band: re-advect 2000 tracers (subset of saved positions) using filtered velocity fields with RK4 integration and GPU grid_sample interpolation
		- For each band: compute MSD exponent γ(j), VACF decay ν(j), and displacement CCDF slope (proxy for α)
		- Save all band results (T_eddy per band, α/γ/ν per band, CCDF slopes) to npz file
		- Print all numerical results per band

- Step 6:
	* sub-task: Plot band-pass wavelet results and test CTRW scaling prediction
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* code_execution_timeout: 300s

	* instructions:
		- Load band results from Step 5
		- Plot α(j) (CCDF slope proxy) vs T_eddy(j) for all 5 bands; fit power-law or linear relationship
		- Plot γ(j) vs T_eddy(j) for all 5 bands
		- Test CTRW prediction: compute β(j) from α(j) via α = 1 + β/(2−β), plot β(j) vs T_eddy(j) and fit β ∝ T_eddy^δ; report δ
		- Plot CCDF of displacement magnitudes for each band on a single log-log figure to show tail behavior
		- Save all plots to the data directory

- Step 7:
	* sub-task: Compare temporal α(t) with band α(j) and build 2D map
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* code_execution_timeout: 300s

	* instructions:
		- Load window-level results from Step 3 and band results from Step 5
		- For each temporal window, identify the dominant wavelet band j_peak(t) as the band whose k0 is closest to k_peak(t)
		- Compare α(t) from Step 3 with α(j_peak(t)) from Step 5 — compute correlation and mean difference
		- Build a 2D scatter/interpolated map of α as a function of (T_eddy, k_peak/k_box) using all available data points from both analyses
		- Save synthesis results (comparison arrays, 2D map data) to npz file
		- Print all numerical comparison statistics

- Step 8:
	* sub-task: Generate synthesis plots
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* code_execution_timeout: 300s

	* instructions:
		- Load synthesis results from Step 7
		- Plot α(t) from temporal windows vs α(j_peak(t)) from wavelet bands as a scatter plot with 1:1 line
		- Plot the 2D map of α as a function of (T_eddy, k_peak/k_box) using a color-coded scatter or interpolated contour plot
		- Plot α(t) overlaid with k_peak(t)/k_box on a dual-axis time series to visually show co-evolution
		- Save all plots to the data directory

- Step 9:
	* sub-task: Write the full Results section of the paper
	* agent in charge: researcher

	* instructions:
		- Summarize all quantitative results from Steps 2–8: displacement quality check, VACF decorrelation, inverse cascade confirmation, stationarity test outcomes, α(t)/γ(t)/ν(t) evolution, α vs k_peak fit parameters, band-pass T_eddy values, CTRW scaling exponent δ, synthesis comparison
		- Interpret the physical meaning of each result in the context of Lévy flight theory, CTRW, and 2D turbulence inverse cascade
		- Discuss whether α ≈ 1.4 from v1 is stationary or drifts, and what drives the drift
		- Discuss the CTRW prediction α = 1 + β/(2−β) and whether it holds across wavelet bands
		- Reference all plots generated in previous steps by description
		- Write in academic paper style with full quantitative detail

name: plan_reviewer
content:
**Recommendations:**

- Step 1 instructions should explicitly state that after copying the template, the engineer must read and understand the existing production loop structure before modifying it, and must preserve all existing spinup logic, RK4 integrator, spectral dealiasing, and forcing calibration code verbatim — only appending output-saving code to the production loop rather than restructuring it. The instructions should also specify that the diagnostics structured array must use numpy structured dtype with named fields (time, E_total, E_rms, omega_rms, T_L, k_peak, d_vv_estimate) and that sim_params.json must record the actual T_L measured at end of spinup, not a hardcoded value.

- Step 1 instructions should specify that the coarsening from 1024 to 256 for velocity and vorticity snapshots must be done using torch.nn.functional.avg_pool2d with kernel_size=4 on the GPU before transferring to CPU for saving, and that the energy spectrum must be computed as the azimuthally-averaged 2D power spectrum using integer wavenumber bins from k=1 to k=N//2, saved at every tracer snapshot time (not just at Eulerian snapshot times).

- Step 2 instructions should clarify that the VACF must be computed using the tracer_velocities array (not finite differences of positions), normalizing by C_vv(0) = mean squared velocity, and that the decorrelation time τ_corr should be defined as the first lag where C_vv drops below 1/e of its initial value, with this value printed explicitly in simulation time units and in units of T_L.

- Step 3 instructions should specify that the McCulloch α estimator must be applied to the distribution of scalar displacement magnitudes |Δr(τ)| (not vector components separately) at each lag, and that the Hill estimator should use the top k = max(50, N//10) order statistics of the displacement magnitude distribution, with the threshold k printed alongside the estimate. The instructions should also note that if fewer than 3 temporal windows can be constructed (e.g., if T_prod is shorter than expected), the step should print a warning and proceed with whatever windows are available rather than failing.

- Step 3 instructions should specify that the MSD power-law fit for γ(t) must be performed over lag range τ ∈ [1, W/4] steps (not the full window width) to avoid boundary effects, and that the VACF algebraic decay fit for ν(t) must use only lags where C_vv > 0.05 to avoid fitting noise. Both fits should use log-log linear regression via numpy polyfit and print the R² value for each window.

- Step 5 instructions should clarify that the Gaussian band-pass filters must be constructed on the 256×256 grid (matching velocity_snapshots.npy resolution), not the original 1024×256 grid, and that the wavenumber magnitudes |k| must be computed correctly for the rfft2 output shape (256, 129) accounting for the asymmetric frequency grid. The re-advection of 2000 tracers should use the first 2000 tracers from tracer_positions.npy at the first available snapshot time as initial conditions, and the RK4 integration should use linear temporal interpolation between consecutive velocity snapshots.

- Step 5 instructions should add that for each band, the CCDF slope (proxy for α) must be estimated by fitting a power law to the upper tail of the displacement magnitude distribution (top 20% of values) using log-log linear regression, and that the fitted slope and its uncertainty (standard error from polyfit) must both be printed and saved. The instructions should also note that if a band has negligible energy (band energy < 1% of total), it should be flagged and skipped rather than producing spurious α estimates.

- Step 6 instructions should specify that the CTRW inversion α = 1 + β/(2−β) must be solved for β as β = 2(α−1)/(α) and that β values outside [0, 1] (which would be unphysical) must be flagged and excluded from the power-law fit β ∝ T_eddy^δ. The fit should be performed in log-log space using numpy polyfit and the exponent δ with its standard error must be printed explicitly.

- Step 7 instructions should specify that the correlation between α(t) and α(j_peak(t)) must be computed as both Pearson and Spearman rank correlation coefficients (with p-values), since the relationship may be monotonic but non-linear. The 2D map should use scipy.interpolate.griddata with method='linear' to interpolate onto a regular (T_eddy, k_peak/k_box) grid, and the interpolated grid bounds and resolution must be printed.

- Step 9 instructions should specify that the researcher must explicitly state the numerical values of all key fitted parameters (a, b, c from α vs k_peak fit; δ from CTRW scaling; Pearson and Spearman correlations from synthesis; ADF and KPSS test statistics and p-values) and must explicitly address any cases where physical predictions were not confirmed by the data, providing alternative interpretations rather than simply omitting negative results.

name: reviewer_response_evaluator
content:
**Recommendations:**

Step 1 instructions should explicitly state that after copying the template, the engineer must read and understand the existing production loop structure before modifying it, and must preserve all existing spinup logic, RK4 integrator, spectral dealiasing, and forcing calibration code verbatim — only appending output-saving code to the production loop rather than restructuring it. The instructions should also specify that the diagnostics structured array must use numpy structured dtype with named fields (time, E_total, E_rms, omega_rms, T_L, k_peak, d_vv_estimate) and that sim_params.json must record the actual T_L measured at end of spinup, not a hardcoded value.

Step 1 instructions should specify that the coarsening from 1024 to 256 for velocity and vorticity snapshots must be done using torch.nn.functional.avg_pool2d with kernel_size=4 on the GPU before transferring to CPU for saving, and that the energy spectrum must be computed as the azimuthally-averaged 2D power spectrum using integer wavenumber bins from k=1 to k=N//2, saved at every tracer snapshot time (not just at Eulerian snapshot times).

Step 2 instructions should clarify that the VACF must be computed using the tracer_velocities array (not finite differences of positions), normalizing by C_vv(0) = mean squared velocity, and that the decorrelation time τ_corr should be defined as the first lag where C_vv drops below 1/e of its initial value, with this value printed explicitly in simulation time units and in units of T_L.

Step 3 instructions should specify that the McCulloch α estimator must be applied to the distribution of scalar displacement magnitudes |Δr(τ)| (not vector components separately) at each lag, and that the Hill estimator should use the top k = max(50, N//10) order statistics of the displacement magnitude distribution, with the threshold k printed alongside the estimate. The instructions should also note that if fewer than 3 temporal windows can be constructed (e.g., if T_prod is shorter than expected), the step should print a warning and proceed with whatever windows are available rather than failing.

Step 3 instructions should specify that the MSD power-law fit for γ(t) must be performed over lag range τ ∈ [1, W/4] steps (not the full window width) to avoid boundary effects, and that the VACF algebraic decay fit for ν(t) must use only lags where C_vv > 0.05 to avoid fitting noise. Both fits should use log-log linear regression via numpy polyfit and print the R² value for each window.

Step 5 instructions should clarify that the Gaussian band-pass filters must be constructed on the 256×256 grid (matching velocity_snapshots.npy resolution), not the original 1024×256 grid, and that the wavenumber magnitudes |k| must be computed correctly for the rfft2 output shape (256, 129) accounting for the asymmetric frequency grid. The re-advection of 2000 tracers should use the first 2000 tracers from tracer_positions.npy at the first available snapshot time as initial conditions, and the RK4 integration should use linear temporal interpolation between consecutive velocity snapshots.

Step 5 instructions should add that for each band, the CCDF slope (proxy for α) must be estimated by fitting a power law to the upper tail of the displacement magnitude distribution (top 20% of values) using log-log linear regression, and that the fitted slope and its uncertainty (standard error from polyfit) must both be printed and saved. The instructions should also note that if a band has negligible energy (band energy < 1% of total), it should be flagged and skipped rather than producing spurious α estimates.

Step 6 instructions should specify that the CTRW inversion α = 1 + β/(2−β) must be solved for β as β = 2(α−1)/(α) and that β values outside [0, 1] (which would be unphysical) must be flagged and excluded from the power-law fit β ∝ T_eddy^δ. The fit should be performed in log-log space using numpy polyfit and the exponent δ with its standard error must be printed explicitly.

Step 7 instructions should specify that the correlation between α(t) and α(j_peak(t)) must be computed as both Pearson and Spearman rank correlation coefficients (with p-values), since the relationship may be monotonic but non-linear. The 2D map should use scipy.interpolate.griddata with method='linear' to interpolate onto a regular (T_eddy, k_peak/k_box) grid, and the interpolated grid bounds and resolution must be printed.

Step 9 instructions should specify that the researcher must explicitly state the numerical values of all key fitted parameters (a, b, c from α vs k_peak fit; δ from CTRW scaling; Pearson and Spearman correlations from synthesis; ADF and KPSS test statistics and p-values) and must explicitly address any cases where physical predictions were not confirmed by the data, providing alternative interpretations rather than simply omitting negative results.

name: review_recorder
content:
Recommendations have been logged.
Number of feedback rounds left: 0.
Now, update the plan accordingly, planner!

       - Load tracer_positions.npy, tracer_velocities.npy, tracer_times.npy, diagnostics.npy
       - Define overlapping windows of width W = 8 T_L, step ΔW = 2 T_L; if fewer than 3 windows can be constructed, print a warning and proceed with available windows rather than failing
       - For each window: apply McCulloch quantile method to scalar displacement magnitudes |Δr(τ)| (not vector components) at lags τ = {0.5, 1, 2} T_L using quantiles p5, p25, p50, p75, p95; compute Hill estimator using top k = max(50, N//10) order statistics and print the threshold k alongside each estimate
       - For each window: fit MSD(τ) power law over lag range τ ∈ [1, W/4] steps using log-log linear regression (numpy polyfit); print R² for each window
       - For each window: fit VACF algebraic decay using only lags where C_vv > 0.05 to avoid noise; use log-log linear regression; print R² for each window
       - Extract d_vv(t) and k_peak(t) from diagnostics averaged within each window
       - Run ADF and KPSS stationarity tests on α(t) and γ(t) time series; print all test statistics and p-values
       - Save all window-level results (α_McCulloch, α_Hill, γ, ν, d_vv, k_peak, window center times) to npz file
     * code_execution_timeout: 1800

- Step 4: Non-Stationarity Analysis — Visualization
     * sub-task: Plot non-stationarity results and α vs k_peak relationship
     * agent: engineer
     * bullet points:
       - Load the window-level results saved in Step 3
       - Plot α(t) and γ(t) vs time with McCulloch and Hill α on same panel, showing temporal evolution
       - Plot ν(t), d_vv(t), k_peak(t) vs time in a multi-panel figure
       - Plot α(t) vs k_peak(t)/k_box (k_box=1) and fit α = a + b × (k_peak/k_box)^c; show fit on plot and print fitted parameters a, b, c with uncertainties
       - Save all plots to the data directory
     * code_execution_timeout: 300

- Step 5: Gaussian Band-Pass Wavelet Analysis — Computation
     * sub-task: Filter velocity fields into Gaussian spectral bands and re-advect tracers
     * agent: engineer
     * bullet points:
       - Load velocity_snapshots.npy (shape N_vel, 2, 256, 256), vel_times.npy, tracer_positions.npy, tracer_times.npy from the data directory
       - Define 5 Gaussian band-pass filters on the 256×256 grid: (k0=25,σ=5), (k0=15,σ=4), (k0=8,σ=3), (k0=4,σ=2), (k0=2,σ=1); compute wavenumber magnitudes |k| correctly for rfft2 output shape (256, 129) accounting for the asymmetric frequency grid
       - For each band: check if band energy < 1% of total energy — if so, flag and skip the band
       - For each band: apply rfft2 on GPU, multiply by G(k)=exp(-(|k|-k0)²/(2σ²)), irfft2 to get filtered velocity fields
       - For each band: compute T_eddy(j) from temporal autocorrelation of band energy, integrating until C_E drops below 0.1
       - Re-advect the first 2000 tracers from tracer_positions.npy at the first available snapshot time as initial conditions; use RK4 integration with linear temporal interpolation between consecutive velocity snapshots and GPU grid_sample interpolation
       - For each band: compute MSD exponent γ(j) and VACF decay ν(j); estimate CCDF slope by fitting power law to upper tail (top 20% of displacement magnitudes) using log-log linear regression; print fitted slope and standard error from polyfit
       - Save all band results (T_eddy, α/γ/ν per band, CCDF slopes and uncertainties) to npz file; print all numerical results per band
     * code_execution_timeout: 3600

- Step 6: Wavelet Analysis — Visualization and CTRW Test
     * sub-task: Plot band-pass wavelet results and test CTRW scaling prediction
     * agent: engineer
     * bullet points:
       - Load band results from Step 5
       - Plot α(j) (CCDF slope proxy) vs T_eddy(j) for all valid bands; fit power-law or linear relationship
       - Plot γ(j) vs T_eddy(j) for all valid bands
       - Test CTRW prediction: compute β(j) from α(j) via β = 2(α−1)/α; flag and exclude β values outside [0, 1] as unphysical; fit β ∝ T_eddy^δ in log-log space using numpy polyfit; print δ and its standard error explicitly
       - Plot β(j) vs T_eddy(j) with the power-law fit
       - Plot CCDF of displacement magnitudes for each valid band on a single log-log figure
       - Save all plots to the data directory
     * code_execution_timeout: 300

- Step 7: Synthesis — Computation and Visualization
     * sub-task: Compare temporal α(t) with band α(j), build 2D map, and generate synthesis plots
     * agent: engineer
     * bullet points:
       - Load window-level results from Step 3 and band results from Step 5
       - For each temporal window, identify dominant wavelet band j_peak(t) as the band whose k0 is closest to k_peak(t)
       - Compare α(t) from Step 3 with α(j_peak(t)) from Step 5: compute both Pearson and Spearman rank correlation coefficients with p-values; print all correlation statistics
       - Build a 2D map of α as function of (T_eddy, k_peak/k_box) using scipy.interpolate.griddata with method='linear'; print interpolated grid bounds and resolution
       - Save synthesis results (comparison arrays, 2D map data) to npz file
       - Plot α(t) from temporal windows vs α(j_peak(t)) from wavelet bands as scatter plot with 1:1 line
       - Plot the 2D interpolated map of α as function of (T_eddy, k_peak/k_box) as color-coded contour plot
       - Plot α(t) overlaid with k_peak(t)/k_box on dual-axis time series
       - Save all plots to the data directory
     * code_execution_timeout: 300

- Step 8: Results Report
     * sub-task: Write the full Results section of the paper
     * agent: researcher
     * bullet points:
       - Summarize all quantitative results from Steps 2–7: displacement quality check values, VACF decorrelation time in simulation units and T_L units, inverse cascade k_peak drift, stationarity test statistics and p-values (ADF and KPSS), α(t)/γ(t)/ν(t) temporal evolution, fitted parameters a, b, c from α vs k_peak fit, band-pass T_eddy values per band, CTRW scaling exponent δ with standard error, Pearson and Spearman correlations from synthesis with p-values
       - Interpret the physical meaning of each result in the context of Lévy flight theory, CTRW, and 2D turbulence inverse cascade
       - Explicitly state whether α ≈ 1.4 from v1 is stationary or drifts, and what drives the drift
       - Explicitly discuss the CTRW prediction α = 1 + β/(2−β) and whether it holds across wavelet bands, including any cases where predictions were not confirmed with alternative interpretations
       - Reference all plots generated in previous steps by description
       - Write in academic paper style with full quantitative detail

name: planner_response_evaluator
content:
**PLAN**

- Step 1:
	* sub-task: Run the 2D turbulence DNS simulation with passive tracer advection
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* code_execution_timeout: 7200s

	* instructions:
		- Copy /home/node/work/projects/levy_flights_2dns_v2/dns_template_1024_ekman.py to codebase/step_1.py verbatim, then read and understand the existing production loop structure before making any modifications
		- Preserve ALL existing spinup logic, RK4 integrator, spectral dealiasing, and forcing calibration code verbatim — only append output-saving code to the production loop, do not restructure it
		- Keep all solver parameters from the template (N=1024, k_force=[20,40], lambda_ekman=0.5, nu_h=3.9e-31, T_spinup=60, dt_snap=0.02); ALL tensor operations must remain on device='cuda'
		- After spinup completes, measure and print actual U_rms, T_L, k_peak; record the actual T_L in sim_params.json (not a hardcoded value)
		- During production loop: coarsen 1024→256 velocity and vorticity using torch.nn.functional.avg_pool2d with kernel_size=4 on GPU before CPU transfer; compute azimuthally-averaged energy spectrum using integer wavenumber bins k=1 to N//2 at every tracer snapshot time
		- Save diagnostics.npy as numpy structured array with named fields: time, E_total, E_rms, omega_rms, T_L, k_peak, d_vv_estimate
		- Save all required files to /home/node/work/projects/levy_flights_2dns_v2/data/: tracer_positions.npy, tracer_velocities.npy, tracer_times.npy, velocity_snapshots.npy, vorticity_snapshots.npy, vel_times.npy, energy_spectrum.npy, diagnostics.npy, sim_params.json
		- Print U_rms, T_L, k_peak after spinup and every 10 T_L during production

- Step 2:
	* sub-task: Verify snapshot quality and confirm inverse cascade development
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* code_execution_timeout: 600s

	* instructions:
		- Load tracer_positions.npy, tracer_velocities.npy, tracer_times.npy, energy_spectrum.npy, diagnostics.npy from the data directory
		- Compute mean tracer displacement per snapshot step using position differences; check it is < 0.3 × 2π ≈ 1.88; print result and flag if violated
		- Compute VACF C_vv(τ) using the tracer_velocities array (not finite differences), normalizing by C_vv(0) = mean squared velocity, at lags τ = 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 steps; report C_vv(τ=1); define τ_corr as first lag where C_vv drops below 1/e and print it in both simulation time units and units of T_L
		- Plot E(k) at t = 0.1, 0.3, 0.6, 1.0 × T_prod on a single figure with 4 subplots (log-log scale), showing k_peak drift from forcing scale toward large scales
		- Plot k_peak(t) vs time to visualize inverse cascade progression
		- Save all plots to the data directory

- Step 3:
	* sub-task: Compute α(t), γ(t), ν(t), d_vv(t), k_peak(t) in overlapping temporal windows

	* code_execution_timeout: 1800s

		- For each window: apply McCulloch quantile method to scalar displacement magnitudes |Δr(τ)| (not vector components) at lags τ = {0.5, 1, 2} T_L using quantiles p5, p25, p50, p75, p95; compute Hill estimator using top k = max(50, N//10) order statistics and print the threshold k alongside each estimate
		- For each window: fit MSD(τ) power law over lag range τ ∈ [1, W/4] steps using log-log linear regression (numpy polyfit); print R² for each window
		- For each window: fit VACF algebraic decay using only lags where C_vv > 0.05 to avoid noise; use log-log linear regression; print R² for each window
		- Extract d_vv(t) and k_peak(t) from diagnostics averaged within each window
		- Run ADF and KPSS stationarity tests on α(t) and γ(t) time series; print all test statistics and p-values
		- Save all window-level results (α_McCulloch, α_Hill, γ, ν, d_vv, k_peak, window center times) to npz file

- Step 4:
	* sub-task: Plot non-stationarity results and α vs k_peak relationship
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* code_execution_timeout: 300s

	* instructions:
		- Load the window-level results saved in Step 3
		- Plot α(t) and γ(t) vs time with McCulloch and Hill α on same panel, showing temporal evolution
		- Plot ν(t), d_vv(t), k_peak(t) vs time in a multi-panel figure
		- Plot α(t) vs k_peak(t)/k_box (k_box=1) and fit α = a + b × (k_peak/k_box)^c; show fit on plot and print fitted parameters a, b, c with uncertainties
		- Save all plots to the data directory

- Step 5:
	* sub-task: Filter velocity fields into Gaussian spectral bands and re-advect tracers

		- For each band: apply rfft2 on GPU, multiply by G(k)=exp(-(|k|-k0)²/(2σ²)), irfft2 to get filtered velocity fields
		- For each band: compute T_eddy(j) from temporal autocorrelation of band energy, integrating until C_E drops below 0.1
		- Re-advect the first 2000 tracers from tracer_positions.npy at the first available snapshot time as initial conditions; use RK4 integration with linear temporal interpolation between consecutive velocity snapshots and GPU grid_sample interpolation
		- For each band: compute MSD exponent γ(j) and VACF decay ν(j); estimate CCDF slope by fitting power law to upper tail (top 20% of displacement magnitudes) using log-log linear regression; print fitted slope and standard error from polyfit

- Step 6:

content: Plan recorded as final. Planning stage complete.
